retrieved_docs.title: Latent Autoimmune Diabetes -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with LADA have residual C-peptide levels, typically between those with T1DM and T2DM. In T1DM, C-peptide is absent at first clinical presentation, and in T2DM, it is often increased. The levels of C-peptide correlate inversely with GADA titers. A stimulated C-peptide has a greater predictive value than a fasting level. The glucagon stimulation test and mixed meal tolerance test (MMTT) have been validated and useful among the methods studied. The former is shorter in duration but associated with transient nausea, while the latter takes a long time but is free of side effects. C-peptide measurement is preferable to insulin measurement as it has a longer half-life, is not subject to first-pass hepatic metabolism, and has steady-state clearance from the circulation. Insulin undergoes first-pass hepatic metabolism and has a much shorter half-life and variable clearance. Exogenously administered insulin can confound results. The MMTT has also been utilized to choose the treatment modality and predict the time to transition to managing insulin. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Treatment / Management -- Continuous glucose monitors
retrieved_docs.content: CGMs have now become the standard of care in diabetes management. These devices are especially important in T1D management, as patients have an increased risk of frequent and severe hypoglycemia and hypoglycemia unawareness. Real-time CGM devices measure glucose every few minutes and automatically transmit glucose data to a receiver or phone application device. The device can also alarm when hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia triggers are met. CGMs collect real-time blood glucose data and trends, allowing patients to anticipate blood glucose levels for the next 30 to 60 minutes and adjust treatment decisions accordingly. Intermittent or flash-type glucose monitoring devices are also available. These devices require patients to periodically scan their sensors with a reader or phone application to obtain blood glucose readings.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrochemiluminescence Method -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Electrochemiluminescence provides highly sensitive and specific measurements, enabling the detection and quantification of analytes with exceptional precision. Signals are displayed as relative light units (RLU), representing the amount of light emitted. [4] The RLU is converted into concentration units by a calibration curve plotted using various analyte concentrations and signals obtained.

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Diagnostic Tests -- Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM)
retrieved_docs.content: Flash blood glucose monitoring (continuous interstitial fluid glucose monitoring) involves applying a water-resistant disposable sensor on the back of the upper arm or abdomen. Depending on the product, the sensor can remain on the patient for 3 to 14 days. The sensor can be scanned with a reader, which displays the current interstitial fluid glucose level and trends over the previous eight hours. CGM machines can store 90 days of glucose data. Data from the CGM device can be shared with family and care providers via a smartphone application; these devices are often capable of sending alarms or alert messages, including during episodes of hypoglycemia. In addition, some CGMs are compatible with insulin delivery devices and can stop insulin delivery if the machine predicts or recognizes a drop in BSL. Some older CGM machines require twice daily finger-prick tests for calibration. However, the more recently introduced devices do not require this calibration.

retrieved_docs.title: Enzyme Linked Immunosorbent Assay -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Once color changes are measured from the assay, the results are graphed either on paper or in software. [1] Typically, the graph compares optical density to log concentration, which gives a sigmoidal curve. Known concentrations give the graph's standard curve, and measurement of unknowns can then occur when sample values are compared to the linear portion of the graphed standard curve. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Treatment / Management -- Blood Glucose Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Monitoring blood glucose is an integral part of T1D management. Monitoring can be accomplished through capillary blood sampling with a glucose meter or CGM systems that provide real-time glucose data via a sensor and reader or smartphone app.

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Diagnostic Tests -- Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM)
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages: In patients with type 1 diabetes mellitus and patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus who require intensive insulin therapy or sulfonylureas, flash monitoring has been demonstrated to be cost-effective when compared to CBG self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG). Interstitial glucose measurements are recorded as frequently as every 5 minutes every hour, which has the benefit of monitoring for hypoglycemia during sleep.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrochemiluminescence Method -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: When implementing electrochemiluminescence systems, factors such as sensitivity, specificity, and cost-effectiveness should be carefully evaluated for each clinical setting, with collaborative input from healthcare practitioners, laboratory professionals, and technicians. Clear and concise communication regarding patient history, test requirements, and interpretation of electrochemiluminescence results promotes accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment decisions, and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Testing Procedures -- Glucose Meters
retrieved_docs.content: These meters use reflectance photometry or electrochemistry to measure the reaction rate or the products' final concentration. Reflectance photometry measures the light reflected from a test pad containing a reagent. In electrochemical systems, the enzymatic reaction in an electrode incorporated on the test strip produces a flow of electrons. [52] The current, directly proportional to the concentration of glucose in the sample, is converted to a digital readout. [53] Large variability has been noted among current meters as to the volume of blood required (0.3 to 1.5 μL), test time (5 to 45 seconds), and the claimed reading range: 30 to 500 mg/dL (1.7 to 27.8 mmol/L) to 0 to 600 mg/dL (0 to 33.3 mmol/L). [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: High sensitive CRP (hsCRP) is another surrogate test that can help distinguish HFN1A MODY from other types of diabetes. This value will be lower in HNF1A MODY (MODY 3), usually less than 0.75mg/L, when compared to other forms of diabetes. [20] Additionally, one can assess for residual beta-cell function. Patients with MODY generally have elevated C-peptide levels when there is hyperglycemia, usually 0.6mg/dL or higher. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Utility of Fructosamine and Glycated Albumin -- Testing Procedures -- Glycated Albumin
retrieved_docs.content: The enzymatic assay (Lucica GA-L kit, Asahi Kasei Pharma, Tokyo, Japan) is easier to use, highly accurate, and automated. [10] First, there is the elimination of endogenous glycated amino acids and peroxide by a ketoamine oxidase, followed by a peroxidase reaction. [11] An albumin-specific proteinase hydrolyzes the glycated albumin. The products of this reaction are oxidized by ketoamine oxidase to hydrogen peroxide, which is then measured quantitatively using a colorimetric method. The albumin concentration is also measured concurrently. The final result is expressed as the ratio of glycated to total albumin. [12] The normal value is around 14%, and it increases to greater than 17% in patients with diabetes mellitus. Values in diabetes mellitus can go as high as 2 to 5 times the upper limit of normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes in Children -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) has become more common in children and adolescents, and measures of "time in range" and glucose variability are likely to be even more valuable than Hemoglobin A1c, although insurance does not universally cover CGM and is not always desired by patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders are closely linked to the development of diabetic hyperosmolar coma, as disturbances in fluid balance and electrolyte concentrations can precipitate the severe hyperosmolar state that characterizes this life‑threatening complication of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2839
retrieved_docs.content: بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes). Patients with type 1 diabetes have a heightened risk of severe peripheral vascular disease and foot complications, which can progress to the need for an above‑knee amputation. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians to implement early screening, aggressive glycemic control, and multidisciplinary foot‑care strategies aimed at preventing major limb loss and improving overall patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2742
retrieved_docs.content: فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes mellitus), indicating that elevated cholesterol levels frequently coexist with this form of diabetes. In addition, ألم الصَدْر (chest pain) is clinically associated with فرط كوليسترول الدم, suggesting that patients who have high cholesterol may present with chest discomfort, which can reflect underlying cardiovascular involvement. The combined relationships imply that individuals with مرض السكري نوع 1 who also develop فرط كوليسترول الدم are at heightened risk for experiencing ألم الصَدْر and related cardiac complications, underscoring the importance of regular lipid monitoring and vigilant assessment of chest pain symptoms in the management of type 1 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2614
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is described as a general clinical state that is interpreted by clinicians (is_interpreted_by) and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications. This indicates that an uncontrolled disease condition determination reflects a concerning overall clinical status and frequently co‑occurs with complicated diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful assessment and targeted management in patients presenting with such uncontrolled conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2076
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic agents for diabetes testing are a class of substances employed to evaluate glucose metabolism and detect diabetes, and tolbutamide is identified as a member of this diagnostic group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), indicating that patients with these forms of diabetes are at increased risk for developing sudden renal impairment. Dehydration (التجفاف) is also clinically linked to acute kidney failure, serving as a common precipitating factor that can exacerbate renal dysfunction in diabetic individuals. Recognizing the interplay among acute kidney failure, diabetes (both type 1 and insulin‑dependent type 2), and dehydration is essential for early identification, prevention, and management of renal complications in these patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2470
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1), low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), and abnormal psychological states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). This interrelationship indicates that individuals with type 1 diabetes are at increased risk of developing acute kidney failure, potentially mediated by hypovolemia, and that the onset of acute kidney failure may be accompanied by psychological disturbances. Recognizing and addressing both volume status and mental health in patients with type 1 diabetes can help prevent or mitigate acute kidney failure and improve overall patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: drugs used in diabetes. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in selecting appropriate combination therapies, recognizing the mechanisms of action of individual agents, and optimizing patient‑centered diabetes care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

retrieved_docs.content: Dipeptidyl peptidase 4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors for blood glucose lowering constitute a therapeutic class of oral antihyperglycemic agents, and both alogliptin and sitagliptin are members of this class.
